---
title: "Syria 2026 Agricultural Shocks — Overview"
subtitle: "Floods and crop fires against the 2025 record-drought baseline"
---

## The analytical spine

The 2026 flooding matters because it is an **anomaly on a desertification
trajectory**, not a recurring hazard. The baseline is drought; the shock is water
(plus crop fires). This study quantifies how much of Syria's tentative 2026 cereal
recovery the floods and fires reversed — measured as **damaged cropland hectares**
translated into production and food-security impact, against the 2025 drought
baseline.

- **2025 — record drought (baseline).** FAO/GIEWS put the cereal harvest near
  **1.2 Mt**, ~60%+ below average and the worst in roughly 60 years; ~16.3M people
  at food-security risk.
- **2026 — tentative recovery.** Harvest projected to rebound toward **~2.3 Mt** on
  more even rainfall, though sowing targets were not fully met.
- **2026 — the shock.** March and June Euphrates flooding submerged thousands of
  hectares of recovering cropland in Raqqa and Deir ez-Zor; a national crop-fire
  signal — Hasakah-dominant — burned cropland across twelve governorates.

::: {.callout-important}
**First-half-2026 case study (DEC-039).** Every headline figure here covers only
floods Mar–Jun and fires May 1 – Jun 12 2026. The summer harvest/fire peak
(Jun–Aug) is unobserved, so all figures are **lower bounds**. Field/expert
verification is the gold standard above remote-sensing self-consistency; re-run
post-harvest for the concluded full-year result.
:::

See [`docs/PRODUCT.md`](../docs/PRODUCT.md) for full intent and the locked research
questions, and [`docs/STRUCTURE.md`](../docs/STRUCTURE.md) for contracts and
validation.

## How to read this report

Every figure that makes a food-security or research-question claim is built **only
from human-validated (Tier-2) damage records** — the validation gate
(`docs/STRUCTURE.md` §6) is enforced in code via `viz.consumable_records()`. The
two pipelines (floods S6, fires S13) had their Tier-2 gates closed by a human
domain expert, not by any agent (DEC-007). Maps and attribution figures carry their
caveats inline.

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| echo: false
# Put the repo root on the path so `schema` and `viz` import regardless of the
# working directory Quarto executes from, and expose it for reading the committed
# validated output tables.
import sys, pathlib
import pandas as pd

REPO_ROOT = None
for _cand in (pathlib.Path.cwd(), *pathlib.Path.cwd().parents):
    if (_cand / "CLAUDE.md").exists() and (_cand / "viz").is_dir():
        REPO_ROOT = _cand
        sys.path.insert(0, str(_cand))
        break
assert REPO_ROOT is not None, "could not locate repo root (CLAUDE.md + viz/)"
OUT = REPO_ROOT / "outputs"
```

## The validation gate, on the real records

The headline figures below are normalised in the food-security layer
(`food_security/impact_layer.py`), which reads **only** validated records. As a
provenance check, here is the gate applied directly to the raw flood and fire
damage tables — the count that reaches every claim-bearing figure.

```{python}
#| label: gate-check
#| echo: true
from schema.damage_schema import read_csv
from viz import consumable_records

flood_recs = read_csv(OUT / "floods" / "flood_damage.csv")
fire_recs  = read_csv(OUT / "tables" / "fire_damage.csv")

for name, recs in (("flood", flood_recs), ("fire", fire_recs)):
    kept = consumable_records(recs)
    print(f"{name:5s}: {len(kept):>3d} validated / {len(recs):>3d} total records "
          f"reach the report")
```

## Headline — validated damaged cropland by governorate

The figure uses the canonical per-AOI headline (union cropland, DEC-015; flood
extent is the peak single event-date so persistent pixels are not double-counted
across dates, DEC-033). It is the same normalised quantity the food-security loss
is computed from.

```{python}
#| label: fig-damage-by-aoi
#| fig-cap: "Validated 2026 damaged cropland (union-headline ha) by governorate and phenomenon. First-half-2026 lower bound."
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
from viz import apply_theme, caveat_footer, CAVEATS, PHENOMENON_COLORS

impact = pd.read_csv(OUT / "food_security" / "impact_by_aoi.csv")
# PHENOMENON_COLORS is keyed by the Phenomenon enum; seaborn's hue holds the
# string values, so map to value-keyed for the palette.
pal = {k.value: v for k, v in PHENOMENON_COLORS.items()}

apply_theme()
order = (impact.groupby("aoi_id")["damaged_ha_headline_union"].sum()
               .sort_values(ascending=False).index)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))
sns.barplot(
    impact, x="aoi_id", y="damaged_ha_headline_union", hue="phenomenon",
    order=order, palette=pal, ax=ax,
)
ax.set_xlabel("Governorate (area of interest)")
ax.set_ylabel("Damaged cropland — union headline (ha)")
ax.set_title("Validated 2026 cropland damage: floods dominate the Euphrates AOIs")
ax.tick_params(axis="x", rotation=35)
for lbl in ax.get_xticklabels():
    lbl.set_ha("right")
caveat_footer(fig, CAVEATS["case_study_2026h1"])
plt.show()
```

```{python}
#| label: headline-numbers
#| echo: false
nat = pd.read_csv(OUT / "food_security" / "impact_national.csv")
tot = nat.loc[nat["aoi_id"] == "STUDY_TOTAL"].iloc[0]
flood_ha = impact.loc[impact.phenomenon == "flood", "damaged_ha_headline_union"].sum()
fire_ha  = impact.loc[impact.phenomenon == "fire",  "damaged_ha_headline_union"].sum()
n_fire_govs = impact.loc[impact.phenomenon == "fire", "aoi_id"].nunique()

from IPython.display import Markdown
Markdown(f"""
**Headline (first-half-2026 case study, lower bound):**

- **Damaged cropland (union headline):** ~{flood_ha:,.0f} ha flood
  (3 Euphrates/Khabur AOIs) + ~{fire_ha:,.0f} ha fire ({n_fire_govs} governorates).
- **Estimated cereal-production loss:** ~**{tot.production_loss_t_headline:,.0f} t**
  (range {tot.production_loss_t_low:,.0f}–{tot.production_loss_t_season_ref:,.0f} t,
  from cropland-definition × temporal-aggregation sensitivity).
- That is **{tot.loss_pct_of_baseline_headline:.2f}%** of the 12-governorate 2025
  baseline (~1.2 Mt national cereal floor) — *incremental* stress on an
  already record-drought context, not a standalone crisis.
""")
```

The loss is dominated by **riverine flooding on the Euphrates** — **Raqqa** is the
single largest production loss, followed by **Hasakah** and **Deir ez-Zor**. A
caution: Hasakah's large flood figure is inflated by the June hectares that
[RQ1](rq1.qmd) flags as a likely SAR harvest artifact, so it is the most uncertain of
the three. Fires are spatially widespread (Hasakah-dominant) but each governorate's
burned cropland is small relative to the flood extent — see
[Food security](food_security.qmd) for the breakdown and per-AOI loss, and the
research-question pages for attribution.

## What the research questions found

- **[RQ1 — Flood attribution](rq1.qmd).** The June Euphrates flood is
  **upstream-sourced** (zero local rainfall, sustained ~1,600 m³/s dry-season
  discharge, ~6× the drought baseline). Natural-vs-managed release is **LOW
  confidence** — GloFAS cannot resolve dam operations; the dry-season plateau is
  *consistent with* managed release but no dam-release fraction is asserted.
- **[RQ2 — Fire attribution](rq2.qmd).** Live ACLED Syria coverage ends 2025-06-13,
  so the 2026 conflict overlay **cannot be computed** (data gap, not a finding). The
  method is demonstrated on the 2025 window: cropland fires sit **no closer** to
  armed conflict than cropland in general → agricultural/seasonal, not
  frontline-concentrated. There is no active armed conflict in the 2026 window.
- **[RQ3 — Damage vs control areas](rq3.qmd).** A **descriptive** overlay only
  (DEC-005). It maps *where* damage falls relative to indicative control zones; it
  makes **no** differential or causal claim about either administration.
